result:
The cat doesn't know it's Tuesday. This seems important somehow, though I can't say exactly why. She's sitting on the windowsill watching a squirrel perform acrobatics on the feeder I filled yesterday, and for her there is no yesterday, no Tuesday, no concept of the little grid humans have laid over time to make it manageable. There is only the squirrel, the sun-warmed glass, the particular itch behind her left ear.

I envy her sometimes. Not the limitations—I'm fond of language, of memory, of the ability to plan a dinner party three weeks out—but the completeness of her attention. When she eats, she eats. When she sleeps, she sleeps so thoroughly that you could carry her across the room and she'd barely stir. Humans sleep while rehearsing tomorrow's meetings. We eat while scrolling. We exist in a constant state of partial presence, our minds scattered across timelines like birds on telephone wires.

I've been thinking about how words themselves are strange. Take "pocket." There's no reason it should mean that little fabric pouch in your pants. It could just as easily mean a kind of weather, or the sound a frog makes, or the feeling of finding a letter you forgot you wrote. Every word is an arbitrary handshake between speakers, a contract we've all silently signed. And yet when I write "pocket" you see it immediately—maybe jeans, maybe a blazer, maybe the small satin one inside a handbag—and this shared hallucination is what we call communication.

My grandfather used to tell me that the best way to learn something was to teach it. I didn't believe him until I tried to explain long division to a neighbor's kid and realized I didn't actually understand it, I just knew how to perform it. There's a difference. Performance is mimicry with confidence. Understanding is being able to rebuild the thing from scratch if you had to. Most of what we think we know is performance. This isn't a moral failing; it's just the nature of living in a world too complex to fully grasp. We trust the bridge will hold. We trust the pilot knows how to fly. We trust that the coffee isn't poisoned. Civilization is built on delegated understanding.

Somewhere right now, someone is falling in love. Someone else is writing a suicide note. A baby is taking her first breath; an old man is taking his last. A teenager is laughing so hard she can't breathe. A dog is being reunited with his owner after six months apart. All of it happening simultaneously, a symphony of extremes we usually don't think about because thinking about it would be unbearable and also, strangely, clarifying. If I remembered every second that I am part of this vast simultaneous experience, I think I would either become a monk or lose my mind. Probably both.

I went for a walk this morning and saw a man watering his lawn. Nothing remarkable about that. But then I noticed he was humming, and the hum was slightly off-key, and he seemed completely unaware that anyone might hear him, and I thought: this is what happiness actually looks like. Not the big curated moments we photograph, but the small accidental songs we sing when we think no one is listening. The private hums. The way you dance a little in the kitchen when a good part of a song comes on. The satisfied sigh after the first sip of coffee. These are the real artifacts of a life, not the vacations or the graduations or the weddings, which are too self-conscious to count as true happiness. Those are performances of happiness, which is fine, but different.

I wonder about the last thing anyone will ever write. Not in a dark way—just as a curiosity. Someday, eventually, the last human sentence will be composed. It might be a grocery list. It might be a poem. It might be a text message that says "be there in 10." Whoever writes it won't know. That's almost always how endings work: you don't know you're doing something for the last time. The last time you picked up your child who was small enough to pick up. The last conversation you had with an old friend before they moved away and the friendship slowly dissolved into holiday cards and then nothing. The last good summer.

This is probably getting too melancholy. Let me pivot. Have you ever noticed that bread, cheese, and wine are basically just rot that humans have learned to love? We've built entire cultures around controlled decay. There's something beautiful in that. Taking the forces of destruction and coaxing them into flavor. Fermentation as philosophy: what if the things falling apart in your life are becoming something delicious, if only you let them sit long enough in the right conditions?

The cat has left the windowsill. The squirrel has won, or lost, or simply moved on—another creature uninterested in Tuesday. My coffee has gone cold, which is fine, because cold coffee is its own kind of good, like a familiar song played slightly slower. I'm running low on words now, and I notice I don't want to end with anything grand. I want to end the way most days end: a little unfinished, a little tired, curious about tomorrow but not too eager, grateful in an unspecific way for all the small weird things—the humming strangers, the warm glass, the slow decay of bread into something better.

That's the word count, I think. Or close enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
